coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles based fluorescent pH sensor with broad-range responding for environmental and live cells applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Bingfang
Su, Yubin
Zhang, Liangliang
Liu, Rongjun
Huang, Mengjiao
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: A nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles (N-CNs) based fluorescent pH sensor with a broad-range responding was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of melamine and triethanolamine. The as-prepared N-CNs exhibited excellent photoluminesence properties with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 11.0%. Furthermore, the N-CNs possessed a broad-range pH response. The linear pH response range was 3.0 to 12.0, which is much wider than that of previously reported fluorescent pH sensors. The possible mechanism for the pH-sensitive response of the N-CNs was ascribed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Cell toxicity experiment showed that the as-prepared N-CNs exhibited low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility with the cell viabilities of more than 87%. The proposed N-CNs-based pH sensor was used for pH monitoring of environmental water samples, and pH fluorescence imaging of live T24 cells. The N-CNs is promising as a convenient and general fluorescent pH sensor for environmental monitoring and bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent pH sensor
Nitrogen-rich functional groups
Carbon nanoparticles
Live-cell image
Environmental monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH detection for environmental water samples
pH-dependent optical properties of the N-CNs
pH detection of environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and the diameter distribution (B) of N-CNs.
XPS survey spectrum (A), C1s XPS spectrum (B), N1s XPS spectrum (C), and FIRT spectrum (D) of the as-prepared N-CNs.

Fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared N-CNs in BR buffers (from pH 3.0 to 12.0) and the calibration curve from pH 3.0 to 12.0 (inset is the photograph of color changes: the pH from left to right were 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0, 10.0, 11.0 and 12.0, respectively) (A); the linear relationship between pH and zeta potential (B).
The possible luminescence mechanism of the as-prepared N-CNs.

Laser confocal fluorescence images of T24 cells after incubation with N-CNs (0.2mgmL−1) and immobilization in pH 5.0 (A), 7.0 (B), and 9.0 (C) PBS buffer (from left to right: fluorescent image excited with a 405nm laser, bright field, and overlap of the corresponding bright field image and the fluorescence image, respectively).
Schematic illustrations for the synthesis process of N-CNs and pH-responsive.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles based fluorescent pH sensor with broad-range responding for environmental and live cells applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key laboratory of regional ecological environment analysis and pollution control of West Guangxi, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Baise University, Baise 533000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key laboratory of regional ecological environment analysis and pollution control of West Guangxi, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Baise University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles (N-CNs) based fluorescent pH sensor with a broad-range responding was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of melamine and triethanolamine. The as-prepared N-CNs exhibited excellent photoluminesence properties with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 11.0%. Furthermore, the N-CNs possessed a broad-range pH response. The linear pH response range was 3.0 to 12.0, which is much wider than that of previously reported fluorescent pH sensors. The possible mechanism for the pH-sensitive response of the N-CNs was ascribed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Cell toxicity experiment showed that the as-prepared N-CNs exhibited low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility with the cell viabilities of more than 87%. The proposed N-CNs-based pH sensor was used for pH monitoring of environmental water samples, and pH fluorescence imaging of live T24 cells. The N-CNs is promising as a convenient and general fluorescent pH sensor for environmental monitoring and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Minute pH changes negatively impact the lives of plants and animals. Therefore, there is considerable concern regarding pH changes in all life forms (Wu et al., 2014). For example, large amounts of sewage discharged into the environment may cause changes in the environmental pH, which not only pollute drinking water, but also have a range of negative health effects (Yang et al., 2013). In the life science, intracellular pH is of great concern to cell, enzyme and tissue activities, which plays a critical role in modulating many biological reactions (Han and Burgess, 2010). Abnormal intracellular pH in living cells can cause improper cell function and growth, resulting in various diseases and neurological disorders (Dennis et al., 2012). Thus, the facile and accurate pH monitoring in the environment and on the cellular level is of increasing importance.

In recent years, fluorescence-based pH measurement techniques, such as organic dyes (Yang et al., 2013), carbon dots (Dennis et al., 2012; Jin et al., 2015), silicon nanodots (Feng et al., 2014), Cu nanoclusters (Liao et al., 2015), and quantum dots (Jia et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2013; Wan et al., 2014), have received increasing attention owing to their high rapidity, sensitivity, and wide applicability to cells. Among them, organic dyes-based pH sensors are efficient and accessible. Unfortunately, successful application of organic dye-based pH sensors is usually limited because of their complex synthesis/lable processes (Dennis et al., 2012; Wan et al., 2014). More recently, considerable attention has been given to fluorescent carbon nanomaterials owing to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence and tunable luminescence emission (Li et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2011; Peng et al., 2012), which are advantageous over the organic dye-based fluorescence probes used for pH detection (Xu et al., 2004). For instance, Sun and co-workers have reported a one-pot hydrothermal method to prepare carbon dots using threonine as carbon source, and their fluorescence was sensitive to pH in the 2.13–9.34 range, a decrease in fluorescence intensity occurred as pH increased (Jin et al., 2015). Shi et al. have proposed a one-pot solid-phase synthesis method to synthesize a high quantum yield of oxygen-rich N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), and used as pH sensor to obtain good linearity in the 3.0–8.0pH range (Shi et al., 2015). Huang and co-workers have developed a facile hydrothermal route to synthesize N-GQDs with pH-sensitive response from 1.81 to 8.96 (Wu et al., 2014). Although these developed techniques are excellent for pH sensing, there are somewhat problems existed, such as the range of response and linear relation for quantification (Wu et al., 2014). Therefore, developing simple, broad range of response and sensitive pH sensor is still challenging.

To date, many strategies for synthesizing carbon dots have been reported (Hu et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2011; Han et al., 2012; Ma et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012). Among them, the hydrothermal route is attractive due to the eco-friendly, the good stability and biocompatibility of as-prepared carbon dots resulting from the O-rich groups on their surface (Zhang et al., 2013). Moreover, protonation and deprotonation of these O-and N-rich groups in aqueous solution is attributed to pH-dependent fluorescence property of carbon dots (Dong et al., 2012). Herein, we reported a facile hydrothermal method to synthesize nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles (N-CNs) using melamine and triethanolamine as carbon source and nitrogen source, respectively. The synthesis process of N-CNs is shown in Scheme 1. The as-prepared N-CNs exhibited blue photoluminescence with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 11.0%. Impressively, the N-CNs possessed distinct pH-sensitive photoluminescence properties in the pH range from 3.0 to 12.0, which is much wider than that of previously reported fluorosensors. The mechanism responsible for the pH-sensitive properties involved photoinduced electron transfer (PET) of aniline to the N-CNs. Moreover, the proposed fluorosensors were successfully applied to detect pH level in real water samples and living cells.

Melamine (C3N6H6, 99.0%) and Triethanolamine (C6H15NO3, 98.0%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals used in the tests were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the reagents were used as received without further purification. Water was purified by employing a Milli-Q plus 185 equip from Millipore (Bedford, MA) and used throughout the work.

In a typical preparation process, melamine (0.3g), triethanolamine (3.0mL) and 10.0mLH2SO4 (1.0M) were mixed in a 50mL Teon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 10h under vigorous stirring. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was cooled down naturally. Next, the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min to dislodge the non-fluorescent deposit and obtain the upper aqueous solution containing the N-CNs. The obtained mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO, Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China) for 48h to remove residual melamine, triethanolamine and fluorescent precursors. The as-prepared N-CNs were dried under vacuum at room temperature for 48h and stored at 4°C for further use.

Absorption measurements were performed with a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses were conducted using KBr pellets on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA) operating at 200kV. X-Ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi Multitechnique Surface Analysis (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were recorded on a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer FL3-P-TCSPC (HORIBA JOBIN JVON, France). Cell imaging was performed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Olympus FluoView FV1000). Cytotoxicity evaluation was performed using an enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader (Multiskan Mk3, Thermofisher).

A sample containing 12mg of N-CNs was diluted to become an aqueous solution of 12mgN-CNs/mL. Then, 20μL of the diluted N-CNs aqueous solution were added into Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer of different pH (3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0, 10.0, 11.0 and 12.0) to give a final volume of 200µL. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensities were recorded in the wavelength range of 390~590nm at an excitation wavelength of 365nm. The fluorescence intensities of N-CNs were used to evaluate the pH value in BR buffer of a different pH. All of the measurements were performed in triplicate, and the standard deviation was plotted as the error bar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH detection for environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples from rainwater, tap water, spring water and river water were filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min. The obtained water samples were then analyzed using the N-CNs based pH sensor.

The cytotoxicity of N-CNs was evaluated by a MTT assay. T24 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate, and 1×104 cells per well were mixed in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, invitrogen). The cells were cultured first for 24h in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37°C. Then, the culture medium was replaced, and the N-CNs solution was added into each well with different concentrations (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, and 12.0mg/mL) and incubated for 48h. Followed by removing the culture medium, fresh medium containing 10μL MTT (5mg/mL) was added to every cell well, which were washed with PBS buffer three times. The cells were further incubated in RPMI 1640 medium for 4h. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 570nm with a Microplate Reader Model. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation:
Cell
viability
( % ) =
OD
treated
OD
control
× 100 %
Where ODcontrol was obtained in the absence of N-CNs, and ODtreated was obtained in the presence of N-CNs.

The T24 cells in RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum were added to each well of a 6-well plate (2mL per well). The cells were cultured for 24h in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2). Then, the culture medium was replaced with 270mL of RPMI 1640 containing 30mL N-CNs (0.5mgmL−1), and cultured for another 24h. Afterwards, the medium was removed and the cells were treated with nigericin (5mg/mL) in PBS (2mL) for 10min. Before imaging, the N-CNs loaded cells were rinsed three times and incubated with 1mL PBS buffer (0.2M) at various pH values (pH=5.0, 7.0, 9.0) for 20min. Cell imaging was performed using confocal laser scanning microscopy with a fixed excitation wavelength of 405nm.

The morphology of the N-CNs was characterized using TEM images. As exhibited in Fig. 1A, the N-CNs was near spherical and well dispersed. The size distribution of the as-prepared N-CNs was mainly in the range of a range 4–7nm with an average size of 5nm in the selected area (Fig. 1B), which was close to that of reported GQDs (Wu et al., 2013). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1A, inset) clearly showed that the lattice spacing was 0.30nm, which was similar to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon (Zhou et al., 2007).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to investigate the composition of the as-prepared N-CNs. The full scan XPS spectrum of the N-CNs showed that C1s, N1s, and O1s signals appeared at 284.2, 399.4, and 530.9eV, respectively ( Fig. 2A). It confirmed the doping of N atoms in the CNs. The high resolution scan of the C 1s region (Fig. 2B) was divided into four component peaks at 285.0eV, 285.6eV, 286.2eV and 288.7eV, which were corresponded to sp
2 C (C–C or C=C), sp
3 C (C–N /C–O), C=N and C=O, respectively (Qu et al., 2013). The XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2C) exhibited four peaks at 399.9eV, 400.1eV, 401.1eV and 401.4eV, which were assigned to C–N, the pyrrole-like N (C–N–C), graphitic N (C=N) and N–H, respectively (Tang et al., 2013). The O 1s spectrum of the N-CNs (Fig. S1) could be decomposed into peaks at 530.7eV, 532.24eV and 532.93eV, which were attributed to –OH, C–O and O=C–O (Chen et al., 2014). The XPS results (Table S1) also showed that the N-CNs had high oxygen content (22.97%), suggesting a low graphitic level for the N-CNs (Shao et al., 2014). It should be noted that the nitrogen content was 9.75%, and N/C atomic ratio was calculated to be 15.61%, which was higher than the various N-doped graphene-based materials previously reported (Shao et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2014). These results revealed that the N-CNs were rich in oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups derived from the dehydration and polymerization of melamine and triethanolamine. FTIR was performed to further confirm the surface functional groups of the N-CNs, and the results are shown in Fig. 2D. As could be seen in Fig. 2D, there were obvious absorption bands at 3427, 2200–1900, 1631 and 1392cm−1, which were corresponded to O–H/N–H, C=N, C=O, and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively (Jia et al., 2012; Lu et al., 2012; Dong et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2013). FTIR spectra indicated that there were various functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, C–N–C and –NH2 on the surface of N-CNs, which was in agreement with the XPS results.

To explore the optical properties of the as-synthesized N-CNs, the UV–vis absorption and excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of N-CNs were investigated. The absorption spectrum of the N-CNs (Fig. S2A) showed a clear absorption band at 268nm and two weak bands at 333 and 365nm. The bands at 268 and 333nm are often attributed to the π→π* transition of C=C and the n→π* transition of C=O bonds of N-CNs, respectively (Eda et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2010). The broad absorption at 365nm may be ascribed to complicated band structure and random energy levels of the carbon dots (Ding et al., 2013), which is in accord with previous research (Li et al., 2012). As shown in Fig. S2B, the emission peak position of the N-CNs was shifted to longer wavelengths, and subsequently the fluorescence intensity decreased remarkably with increasing excitation wavelength, which suggested that the N-CNs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. This is also similar to that had been reported for carbon dots prepared by other methods (Sun et al., 2006; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009). Very bright blue luminescence could be observed under the optimal emission peak at 440nm (Fig. S2A, inset) with an absolute quantum yield of 11.0% (Fig. S3), which was higher than the reported quantum yield of CQDs (2.3%) (Ding et al., 2013). In addition, the decay curve was found to fit best with a two-exponential function with a lifetime of 3.9ns (Fig. S4), which indicated that the possible luminescence mechanism involved the radioactive recombination of excitations (Anilkumar et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2014). The nanosecond lifetime of the N-CNs suggested their potential for biological applications.

The effects of UV exposure, ionic strength, and pH on the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared N-CNs were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CNs showed little change under continuous irradiation (365nm) with a Xe lamp for 30min (Fig. S5), which confirmed that the N-CNs had excellent resistance to photobleaching. To further confirm the stability of N-CNs, the fluorescence intensity in the presence of different ionic strengths were measured. Fig. S6 shows a slight change in the fluorescence intensity even the concentrations of NaCl up to 2.0M. The fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared N-CNs decreased with increasing pH, which indicated that the N-CNs possessed pH-sensitive properties (Fig. S7). These results suggested that N-CNs had great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent optical properties of the N-CNs
ce:section.ce:para: Inspiring by pH-sensitive properties of the N-CNs, the range of response region was explored, and the results are shown in Fig. 3A. As exhibited in Fig. 3A, a clear decrease of the fluorescence intensity could be observed as the pH increased from 3.0 to 12.0. As expected, the color changes of the as-prepared N-CNs in BR buffer solution were from blue to pale blue (Fig. 3A, inset), which could provide a supplementary signal to estimate the pH. Additionally, the pH response of the fluorescence behavior of the N-CNs exhibited excellent reversibility. As showed in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared N-CNs significantly reverted as before with the decrease of pH from 12.0 to 3.0. Even after 10 cycles of changing the pH from 3.0 to 12.0 and then from 12.0 to 3.0 using NaOH and HCl solution to adjust pH, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CNs showed little change under the same conditions. These results suggested that the N-CNs should have basic and acidic sites relevant to the photoluminescence emission (Pan et al., 2010), which was further verified by zeta potential. Fig. 3B shows that a linear relationship (y=−2.58pH+11.95, R2=0.98, y represents the zeta potentials of the N-CNs in different pH buffers) existed between the zeta potential and pH values with an isoelectric point of 4.63, which is analogous to the properties of amino acids (Wu et al., 2013). Moreover, a linear calibration was achieved using the fluorescence intensity of the N-CNs versus pH values when the pH were in a range of 3.0–12.0 (R 2=0.985), which illustrates that the pH sensor could be used for quantitative measurements in a broad response region. Compared with some previously reported fluorescent pH sensor, the response region of the N-CNs was obviously wider as shown in Table S2.

To gain more insight into the possible mechanism of the pH-sensitive fluorescence of the N-CNs, UV–vis spectra were performed. As exhibited in Fig. S9, no obvious change could be observed in the UV–vis spectra of the N-CNs with the pH value increasing from 5.0 to 9.0, which illustrates that the absorption peak at 365nm was not red-shifted. Thus, we inferred that the fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared N-CNs at high pH values might not be attributed to aggregation (Wang et al., 2015). Based on the above observation, a possible mechanism for the pH-sensitive fluorescence of the N-CNs based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) was schematically proposed in Fig. 4. At high pH value, PET from the aniline to the N-CNs becomes feasible owing to the aniline lone pair (Daly et al., 2015). As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the excited electrons in the N-CNs could jump from a fundamental electronic state S0 to the first excited singlet state S1, and then, the electrons could transfer to the HOMO of the aniline because the calculated HOMO of the aniline is higher than that of the N-CNs (Hutt et al., 2014; Daly et al., 2015). Afterwards, the electrons drop back to S0 resulting in the fluorescence quenching. In contrast, the aniline lone pair has been shown to be unavailable for charge transfer and the locally excited state was restored when protonation occurs (Hutt et al., 2014). In this case, the excited electrons in S1 went directly back to S0. Meanwhile, some energy was emitted in the form of photons (Fig. 4a). As a result, the fluorescence of the N-CNs exhibited “turn-on”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH detection of environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the proposed N-CNs based fluorescence pH sensor in complex matrixes, real sample analysis was challenged by environmental water samples including rain water, tap water, spring water and river water. All results are shown in Table S3. As confirmed by Table S3, the result obtained from the present method were reliable when comparing with a pH meter, which proves that the N-CNs-based fluorescence pH sensor could be utilized as an excellent fluorescent pH indicator for real water samples.

To explore the biological applications of the proposed N-CNs based fluorescence pH sensor, the cytotoxicity of the N-CNs was firstly evaluated using T24 cells through MTT assay. The cells viability was estimated to be greater than 87% upon treatment with a high dose of N-CNs of 1.2mg/mL for 24h (Fig. S10), thereby indicating the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the N-CNs for living cells. Hence, the N-CNs are suitable for in vitro and in vivo applications.

Based on the above observations, the laser confocal fluorescence images of T24 cells were carried out to further assess the application of the N-CNs for imaging in living cells. As shown in Fig. 5, a blue emission from the intracellular region, under the 405-nm excitation, could be observed (Fig. 5A–C). This suggested that the N-CNs had entered the cells and labeled both the cell membrane and the cytoplasm of the T24 cells owing to their small size. More interestingly, the N-CNs-loaded T24 cells exhibited significant blue fluorescence at pH 5.0 (Fig. 5A), and the possible reason was most likely that the basic external condition induced H+transport to live cells, which resulted in brighter fluorescence of N-CNs-loaded cells. (Bagar et al., 2009; Hou et al., 2013; Jin et al., 2015). Notably, the fluorescence brightness of T24 cells became weaker as the pH increased (Fig. 5B and C). Additionally, the overlay images of the confocal fluorescence images also exhibited the pH response of the N-CNs in T24 cells by fluorescence brightness. All of these results demonstrated that the as-prepared N-CNs had very good biocompatibility, and were desirable optical pH probes for monitoring the pH changes in live cells.

In conclusion, a facile hydrothermal method was applied to prepare nitrogen-rich functional groups carbon nanoparticles using melamine and triethanolamine as carbon source and dopant, respectively. The as-prepared N-CNs with excellent fluorescence properties were able to resist high ionic strength, acids, and bases. More interestingly, the N-CNs possessed a broad-range pH-sensitive behavior, and a linear relationship was achieved between the fluorescence intensity and pH values from 3.0 to 12.0. Consequently, the N-CNs as a fluorescent pH indicator was used to measure pH values of environmental water samples. Notably, the cell toxicity experiment showed that the as-prepared N-CNs had low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the as-prepared N-CNs. The successful application of the N-CNs for pH imaging of live T24 cells was achieved, proving that the N-CNs based pH indicator could monitor intracellular pH value via a change in the fluorescence brightness. Thus, the as-prepared N-CNs could be used as eco-friendly sensor for bioimaging applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Nos. 21405025, 21575031), and the Natural Science Foundations of Guangxi Province (No. 2014GXNSFBA118047, 2015GXNSFDA139006) as well as BAGUI Scholar Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2016.04.003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
